data.title: Oil May Be Heading For A Breakout
data.description:
Oil has been the one commodity these days that can't seem to catch a bid, but that may be about to change.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) have been among the hottest commodities globally, along with plenty of other metals. Of course, oil faces supply and demand issues more than some metals, and that could be one reason crude oil prices (CO1:COM, CL1:COM) have lagged.

Technicals Are Improving

From a technical point of view, oil is showing significant improvement after finding long-term support around $55 and, more recently, making a solid move higher. Oil has cleared a downtrend that started back in July, and is now testing a resistance level at $61. A breakout and push beyond $61 could help propel oil back to around $65, at least in the near term, and, more importantly, be the start of something more.

Additionally, momentum has improved as measured by the relative strength index. The recent "breakout" in the momentum indicator may signal that, after a long struggle, oil is finally building the momentum to push higher.

Crude oil is also very close to breaking above its 200-day moving average, which, for the time being, has been a region the commodity has struggled to move beyond on several occasions since 2025.

Positioning Has Turned Bearish

Over the last year, non-commercial traders have seen their long positions fall to their lowest levels in some time, while short positions have been gradually building. Non-commercial traders betting on the price declining may be right, but it would also mean oil would probably need to fall below $55. Again, the technical chart shows that the support level has been tested several times over the past year, with no break yet. If Oil does start to build some momentum, the shorts could find themselves needing to cover, and with long positions so low, it is possible that oil could eventually squeeze higher.

Trend-following systematic funds, such as commodity trading advisors, are likely short oil at this point, given that the 20-day moving average is below the 120-day moving average. Typically, these funds use moving averages, such as the 20-day and 120-day, to determine the trend. However, more recently, the 20-day moving average has started to turn higher. If it crosses above the 120-day moving average, it could trigger these CTA funds to start unwinding their short positions and eventually go long.

It isn't a hard-and-fast rule, because every fund can differ, but it can serve as a rule of thumb. Since the beginning of 2025, when the 20-day moving average fell below the 120 moving average, non-commercial short positions have been trending higher, while long positions have been declining.

Risks To The Bullish Thesis

One issue for oil is the dollar (DXY). Typically, a stronger dollar pushes oil prices lower, and a weaker dollar makes them higher, but this is not a certainty.

On a 1-year rolling correlation, the relationship between them has been inverse; however, more recently, it has turned positive, with oil and the dollar showing a positive correlation, meaning they have at times risen together, such as in 2022.

So it remains possible that oil and the dollar can rise together, though it is not common.

Other risks for oil at this point are obviously supply. With leadership changes in Venezuela and prospects for more supply coming online, it could help drive oil prices lower, especially if it happens sooner than expected. Risk of a global slowdown or recession is always present, even though, currently, a recession, at least in the U.S., does not appear likely, given the recent strong third-quarter GDP report.

The technicals do appear to be improving for oil, and that has put it on the cusp of breaking out and moving higher. It isn't there yet, and still has work to do, but with gold and plenty of other commodities running, it may be oil's turn soon enough.

data.title: CSX Corporation Q4: Lackluster End To The Year, But 2026 Looks To Be Better
data.description:
Introduction

CSX Corporation (CSX) reported what I would consider a lackluster end to the year, with a bit more optimistic outlook for 2026 on the back of promising cost-cutting initiatives rather than a better, broader outlook, which helped its shares pass the subpar 2025 performance in hopes of better profitability is yet to come.

I am not too keen on adding CXS to my portfolio just yet, as I still find it a bit expensive, but the cash flow generation looks to be very promising, and will keep an eye out going forward.

By The Numbers

Starting from the top, revenue came in at around $3.51B, essentially flat y/y (-0.8%) and $30m below estimates. Such weak performance came from “lower merchandise volume and reduced export coal revenue.” Some of the negatives were offset by an increase in intermodal revenue growth and higher fuel pricing/fuel surcharges.

Intermodal volumes rose 5%, while revenue was up 7% on new business. Coal volumes were up slightly by 1%. So, overall, a lackluster top-line performance.

On to the company’s profitability and efficiency, operating income came in at $1.11B, down from $1.21B a year ago. And EPS came in at $0.39 vs $0.42 a year ago, affected by $50m in severance and other tech costs. Operating margins overall were up around 30bps from last year.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. CSX finished the year with around $674m in cash, equivalents, and ST investments against $18B in long-term debt, which may look like it is in trouble, but railroads usually maintain leverage anywhere between 2.5x-3.5x net debt/EBITDA, and for the full year 2025, we can see that the company’s net debt/EBITDA is around 3x, which is right at the midpoint of the averages, and consistent with previous quarters. Additionally, for the full year, the company’s interest coverage ratio was 5.3x, which is well above the 2x that analysts consider safe. So, it may look like a lot of debt, but it’s manageable. Railway companies have massive capex needs to maintain tracks, locomotives, and other infrastructure.

Unfortunately, the management did not delve into specific expectations for Q1 ’26, but they did give us some color on what they expect to see in terms of spending and overall vibes for 2026. The management said during the call that they expect to see “low single-digit growth” in revenues, and capital expenditures to be less than $2.4B, which in turn supports the management’s view that the company will see a decent 200bps-300bps operating margin expansion. Additionally, the management expects to see around 50% free cash flow growth compared to 2025, so free cash flow will be at least around $2.5B, and probably closer to $2.7B given the lower capex spending and margins expansion.

Overall, nothing particularly exciting regarding the company’s Q4 and full year-2025 performance. I think the reason the company’s share price is up close to 5% as of writing this review is the decent improvements projected in 2026 operations. Analysts may have already shifted their focus to the future and really liked the more than 50% increase in free cash flow for 2026, as well as a decent operating margin expansion.

Comments On The Outlook

The management didn’t sound very convincing regarding the growth factors based on the overall environment going into 2026, describing the macroeconomic environment as featuring “soft demand,” and no meaningful recovery is expected. The subdued industrial activity, coupled with persistent headwinds in autos, housing, and exports, makes them look internally to see how they can get past what is essentially out of their hands. And how they chose to do it is through operational efficiencies, cost discipline, and productivity, which, as I mentioned above, should lead to 200bps-300bps margin expansion. That is the only thing the company can do.

CSX has to buckle down and weather the storm and make sure it is improving internally, so that improvements don’t rely solely on revenue growth to lead improvements. That is going to come eventually, as with all the cycles. Sometimes there’s uncertainty when it comes to demand, like right now, and then other times these pass, and demand comes back roaring. CSX is geared very well to live through this softening environment, and in the future, when the company is much leaner and more profitable, and demand picks back up, the company is going to perform very well. Even in 2026, the company’s cash flow is going to grow substantially, which will help it expand in the future through more acquisitions.

With the subdued freight volumes, intermodal should be the one to help pick up the slack. This segment is seen as the main driver of the 2026 recovery due to rising driver costs, environmental pressure, and congestion concerns, pushing shippers to reconsider their dependence on long-haul transportation. I expect to see CSX’s intermodal revenue to consistently deliver mid-single-digit growth for the rest of the year, offsetting the other segment’s lackluster outlook.

Of course, there are still other macro risks that could derail (no pun intended) the company’s outlook for 2026. Trump continues to be rather unpredictable on when he decides to announce additional tariffs, but very predictable when it comes to walking the threats back once he announces some sort of additional tariff. The talk of tariffs is back on the table now since no country supports Trump’s need to take over Greenland. So, future supply chain disruptions are still more than a possibility.

Additionally, industrial production and housing are seen as rather weak going forward, which caps volumes. Interest rate decisions going forward will be key for recovery, and we don’t know who is going to be in charge after Fed Chair Jerome Powell steps down, but I hope that it remains as independent as ever and will not be politically influenced.

Should You Invest Right Now?

Unfortunately, as much as I would like to get away from the exposure regarding AI and anything related to it, I don’t see myself buying CSX right now. I ran a quick DCF calculation to give me an idea of what a fair target for CSX would be, and those assumptions are still too optimistic if we compare them to the analysts’ expectations. Below you can see the breakdown.

Revenue is probably fine for now, but operating margins are too high because the company is not going to reach such numbers any time soon, unless its cost-cutting initiatives are very good. Nevertheless, these are the EPS projections we got.

And with these EPS projections, we get a fair value of around $32 a share.

Therefore, I am not in a hurry just yet, but I will keep an eye out for any companies to help me diversify my AI exposure.

data.title: Fed To Hold Steady As We Await The President's Decision
data.description:
President’s pick overshadows Chair Powell

Wednesday’s FOMC meeting is very likely to see monetary policy left unchanged after 75bp of interest rate cuts spread over the previous three meetings. The fact that growth is strong, unemployment is low, equity markets are close to all-time highs and inflation is above target all argue for a pause. Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s robust defence of the central bank’s independence on 11 January, in response to ongoing pressure from the President to lower rates, confirms it. There will be dissent, but it will likely be restricted to arch-dove Stephen Miran and Chris Waller, who remains in the mix as the prospective next Fed Chair.

With just 1bp of a potential 25bp cut priced and only 8bp priced by the March FOMC meeting, the press conference will likely be of little market consequence. The focus will be on President Trump’s imminent nomination for the new Fed Chair, the upcoming data, and whether that person can corral the rest of the committee into further cuts.

Further Fed rate cuts remain likely

Our house view has been that the Fed will cut rates at the March and June FOMC meetings, but the clear risk is that will be delayed by perhaps three months. To deliver a March rate cut, the Fed’s dual mandate will need to come under additional pressure quickly. The tariff impact on inflation has been more muted than expected, and the post-government shutdown numbers provided clear downside surprises, but it would probably require two consecutive drops in employment at the January and February jobs reports to get a majority of members backing it.

Nonetheless, we do still have a conviction view of two further rate cuts with the risks skewed in favour of additional action rather than fewer cuts. Monetary policy is still slightly restrictive and although the growth story is firm, we expect the Fed to be in a position to move policy to a neutral setting given the risks that the jobs data continue to lose momentum.

Federal government worker layoffs resulted in non-farm payrolls falling 173,000 in September. There were gains in November and December of 56k and 50k, respectively, but Chair Jerome Powell’s assertion that the Bureau of Labor Statistics is likely overstating jobs growth by around 60k per month means effectively that employment has stalled. Moreover, outside the government, leisure & hospitality and private education & healthcare services sectors, the US economy has lost jobs in seven of the past eight months.

Fed funds target rate upper band (%)

Concentration risk

The K-shaped story also hints at vulnerabilities. The top 20% of households by income continue to spend strongly, boosted by high incomes and soaring wealth, while the bottom 60% are struggling as concerns about job security and the potential for tariff-induced price hikes sap sentiment. Similarly, in the corporate sector, business capex outside of tech has contracted for four quarters in a row. But investment in computing and software is up 19% year-on-year, which means overall business capex continues to rise.

The concentration of growth in relatively small but fast-growing sectors signals that, from a risk management perspective, it makes sense to get policy to neutral until there starts to be strength in breadth.

Delayed inflation data confirms that tariffs are not having the immediate impact on prices that most feared. That is not to say we can’t rule out that it leads to higher prices eventually. But the fact it is coming through so slowly gives more opportunity for falling energy costs, slowing housing rents and weaker wage growth to mitigate and allow inflation to continue trending down towards 2%. This will give the Fed greater scope to act.

The Fed will see positives coming from the bills-buying programme so far

Since the Fed re-ignited its bills-buying programme, some $65bn has been added. Total bills holdings are now at almost $260bn. Over the same period, some $20bn of MBS has rolled off the balance sheet. Latest data also show a rise in government bond holdings. All in all, in net terms, there has been a $55bn build in the Fed’s holdings of securities since mid-December 2025. That has been partly offset by a rise in the Treasury cash balance through tax inflows. It leaves bank reserves at around $3tr.

And, importantly, repo conditions have calmed. The Fed will be happy with this outcome, as the genesis of the renewed bills-buying programme has been to calm the perception of liquidity tightness that had emerged. That said, the effective fed funds rate remains elevated relative to where it was. It had been 8bp above the fund rate floor. It’s now 14bp above. That may not seem like much, but it is an irritation for the Fed. In fact, it was this drift higher that really prompted the Fed into bills-buying-action. Ideally, the Fed would like to see the effective fund rate drift lower again.

As it is, the effective funds rate is just 1bp below the rate paid on excess reserves, which in fact should act as a ceiling for the effective funds rate. The Fed will still likely declare recent developments as positive, and there has certainly been a net calming repo circumstance. Likely, no further action will be required in this space at this juncture. The policy will remain one of building the bills holdings while reducing the MPS holdings and keep the government bond holdings broadly as is (total Fed holdings are currently $6.2tr, a little over half of which is in government bonds).

Fed could provide the dollar a little support

Wednesday’s FOMC meeting could provide the dollar with a little support as Fed Chair Jerome Powell tries to explain the pause. Given the recent performance of both US asset markets and activity, he will struggle to argue that financial conditions are restrictive and need to be loosened. This could pour cold water on the notion of a second Fed rate cut and this would lift the dollar against the low yielders like the yen and the euro. Instead, the next macro leg lower in the dollar will likely have to emerge from poor data rather than Fed speak.

This Fed meeting comes at a time when the dollar is a little offered. We are not big fans of the story of capital exodus from the US for geopolitical reasons. But strong portfolio flows into emerging markets – typically from USD accounts – are a key theme weighing on the dollar. A Fed going slow on easier policy could dent some of those flows at a time when positioning is getting quite extreme. In all, we favour EUR/USD bouncing around in a 1.16-18 range through February, while a less dovish Fed could be one more catalyst for USD/JPY to trade through 160.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Alphabet Q4 2025 Earnings Preview: Relating Expectations With OLS Model
data.description:
Setting The Stage

In my last coverage, I reiterated my bullish stance on Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) and why I believed it was a better investment over Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) using an OLS regression model. Since that analysis, which was barely a month ago, GOOG has gained more than 9%, beating the S&P 500’s (SP500) 2%, aligning with my bullish thesis.

As the company prepares to report its Q4 2025 earnings on 4th February 2026, investors are faced with a pivotal moment in the AI-driven re-rating narrative. Over the last one year, the stock has delivered a solid performance, soaring by more than 65% and beating the S&P 500 significantly.

This performance comes alongside accelerating Google Cloud growth and resilient advertising. For example, in Q3 2025, Google Cloud saw a 34% YoY growth in revenue to $15.2 billion. Further, the cloud division saw an 82% YoY growth in backlog to about $155 billion in Q3 2025. Alongside these notable highlights in the cloud business is a resilient advertising business where Google Search revenue grew by about 15% YoY to approximately $57 billion, with a similar growth rate observed in the YouTube revenue.

With its current momentum, Q4 2025 is a critical point, as it will provide insights on how the management views 2026 and their approach to the year, especially on key issues like a continuation of heavy CapEx outlay, among other key aspects. As for me, I expect a strong quarterly performance, which is likely to sustain the company’s current momentum. As such, I reiterate my Buy rating.

Consensus Estimates In Perspective

As the earnings release date (February 4) approaches, the consensus revenue estimates stand at $111.43 billion, marking a 15.5% YoY growth. Looking at these estimates, they are consistent with the company’s recent financial trajectory, where it has seen more than eight successive quarters of double-digit revenue growth. This implies that the company is expected to continue with its strong revenue momentum in Q4. This upbeat view is further solidified by the 52 upward annual revenue revisions versus 1 downward revision over the last 3 months.

With the key drivers being cloud computing and advertising, the macro catalysts seem strong, and I believe the consensus estimates are achievable. For example, the cloud market is projected to grow by a CAGR of about 15.7% beyond 2026, which marks a solid market tailwind. I see a similar solid growth projection in the digital advertisement market, which is estimated to expand by a CAGR of more than 13% from 2026 to 2035. This tells you that this company is serving in high-growth markets, and therefore its growth momentum is likely to last in the long run.

Looking at the EPS, the consensus estimates are $2.64, a YoY growth of 23%. Just like the topline, this estimated growth is in line with its current momentum. For about 10 successive quarters, GOOG has seen consistent double-digit growth in EPS. While I believe this company can sustain its EPS momentum, investors should watch if its heavy CapEx could start weighing more noticeably on margins. Either way, this could have little to no impact on price returns given the results of the OLS model, which I will dig deeper into shortly.

Notably, GOOG has beaten the consensus revenue estimates over the last three quarters with an average surprise of about 2%. Assuming this trend, I estimate revenue of about $114 billion in Q4 2025. More interestingly, the company has beaten the EPS estimates for more than ten successive quarters.

Although the last three EPS surprises average at 23.9%, I will assume a conservative EPS beat of about 5% to estimate my EPS target, being alive to the fact that heavy CapEx could impact profitability. With this assumption, I expect an EPS of $2.77 in the upcoming quarterly report. Generally, I expect a strong Q4 2025 characterized by solid growth and earnings beat.

Relating Q4 2025 With The OLS Model: Deeper Insights

In previous analysis (whose link I provided in the introduction paragraph), I regressed quarterly returns against YoY revenue growth, changes in operating margins, and CapEx-to-revenue. This provided a quantitative lens where Q4 could create alpha or disappoint. I will reflect on the Q4 expectations alongside the model's output.

First off, revenue growth had a beta coefficient of 1.82 and a P-Value of 0.000. The P-value being below 0.05, which was the confidence interval, meant that it was statistically significant. On the other hand, the beta coefficient meant that for every 1% higher revenue growth, the stock return grows by about 1.82%. With a consensus YoY growth of 15.5%, it follows that the overall outlook is bullish. Therefore, if total revenue surprises above $113 billion, the high revenue beta could fuel a possible double-digit rally post earnings. Conversely, any revenue deceleration would punish the stock more severely.

For the operating margin, the beta coefficient is 1.35, with a p-value of 0.004, an indication of its statistical significance. Here, I take a closer look at how heavy AI spending could impact profitability. It is likely that operating margins could face pressure if cloud gross margins don’t expand further to offset the effects of the heavy outlay. I don’t rule out positive surprise potential if AI monetization begins to offset costs faster. Here, I believe a 100-200 bps margin beat could add meaningful lift given the beta, but compression might mute upside.

Lastly is the CapEx intensity, where a beta of -0.42 shows a negative correlation with price returns, but a p-value of 0.25 being above the 0.05 confidence interval shows no statistical significance. This implies that the market is tolerant of Alphabet’s CapEx cycles. The implication for its Q4 is that this forgiveness remains a key hedge. That is to say, even if CapEx remains elevated, it is unlikely to trigger a sharp downside unless accompanied by a revenue miss and provided that margins remain stable. Nevertheless, guidance on the 2026 CapEx trajectory remains crucial because any signs of peaking intensity would catalyze FCF inflection and a possible multiple expansion because margins would grow in subsequent quarters.

Above all, with an R-square of 0.68 explaining 68% of the quarterly return variation explained by the above fundamentals, it follows that there is solid room for market sentiments. With this in mind, I am turning to the MACD indicator to weigh the current market sentiments. Looking at the MACD, it is currently above the signal line, both of which are upward trending, an indication of strong bullish market sentiments.

With a strong Q4 expectation, should the company beat estimates, I see this momentum solidifying, hence sustaining the current market sentiments.

What To Look For In The Q4 Report

I have several areas of interest in the upcoming report, three of which I would like to recommend for investors. First off, we should pay close attention to the company’s Google Cloud backlog and margins path. With a backlog of about $155 billion in Q3 2025, any updates on conversion rate or 2026 guidance could validate a multi-year growth story.

Another critical area of interest is the AI monetization signals. We should set our sights on Gemini adoption metrics, enterprise wins, or even new AI-driven search features. This would offer insights into the rationale behind the heavy AI expenditure. Quick adoption would justify the expenditure, potentially solidifying the positive market sentiments.

The last point of interest is the CapEx and FCF outlook. I am interested in 2026 FY guidance on these two metrics. As mentioned above, peak spending would signal FCF inflection and a possible journey to sustainable margin growth.

Conclusion

I expect a strong Q4 2024 characterized by revenue and earnings beats, thus sustaining the current stock momentum. The overall market sentiments are positive, and with the possible solid Q4, I foresee a strong rally post earnings. As such, I maintain my bullish stance with a Buy rating.

data.title: Intel: I Like The Demand Story, I Don't Like The Margin Story (Downgrade)
data.description:
Expectations for Intel Corporation (INTC) were elevated heading into Q4 earnings, particularly for the Data Center and AI segment. This is the first time in many years that I have seen the DCAI segment constrained by supply and not demand.

The Street was modeling roughly $4.43B of revenue for the DCAI segment. Intel obliterated expectations after delivering $4.7B, up 8.9% YOY, noting high demand for its server CPU, the Granite Rapids (Xeon 6).

Unfortunately, the market looked past the demand point and decided to focus on the supply issue. In Q4, buffer inventory was mostly depleted, and the wafer mix shift toward server production would not reach a high output until late Q1. This led to a very soft Q1 2026 revenue and gross margin guidance, below the Street's expectations.

Even though shares are 15% cheaper than yesterday, I decided not to buy the dip yet. Below, I explain why.

Expectations Were High Heading Into Q4 Earnings

In my view, the market had high expectations for Intel's data center AI segment. Interestingly enough, the company obliterated expectations, although disappointed from a guidance perspective.

The Street projected $4.43B in revenue for the data center AI segment. Results showed $4.7B, up 8.9% YOY.

Within DCAI, some analysts (KeyBanc) were focused on the demand for Intel's server CPUs, particularly the Granite Rapids (Intel’s Xeon 6 server CPU line).

Well, it turns out that KeyBanc was right, although the market didn't react positively.

Management confirmed that the buffer inventory was depleted, and the wafer mix shift toward servers won’t come out of the fab until late Q1. As a result of the supply constraints, the revenue guidance for Q1 2026 was quite disappointing. Intel guided Q1 revenue $11.7–$12.7B and non-GAAP EPS $0.00. The Street was looking for $12.51B in revenue and $0.05 in EPS for the quarter.

On top of that, CFO David Zinsner noted the following remarks during the Q4 earnings call, which didn't help improve the sentiment (emphasis added):

Revenue would have been meaningfully higher if we had more supply. While the market continues to benefit from more power-efficient CPUs, stimulating a refresh cycle, all indicators point to the growing and essential role CPUs will play within hyperscale and enterprise AI data centers as inference-driven AI usage expands.

According to the call, supply should improve from Q2 onward. The problem that I see is that Intel’s missed units are likely to become permanently lost if customers qualify alternatives. And, given Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) and Nvidia's (NVDA) dominance in the data center space, I believe there are plenty of better alternatives out there.

Margins Are Shrinking, And Yields Are Still Below Targets

The next metric that disappointed investors was gross margins. Again, the company obliterated the Q4 gross margin expectation, after reporting 37.9% vs 36.5% guided.

I believe the beat had to do with lower inventory reserves than initially expected.

However, the guidance for Q1 was a big disappointment. Intel guided Q1 non-GAAP gross margins of 34.5%. That's a 340 bps sequential decline!

Zooming out, the long-term trend in gross margins is not particularly attractive.

I believe the main headwind to margins is the yield of the new 18A process node. Back in Q3, CFO David Zinsner made some remarks that made me sweat during the earnings conference call (emphasis added):

Yields are -- what I would say, the yields are adequate to address the supply, but they are not where we need them to be in order to drive the appropriate level of margins. And by the end of next year, we'll probably be in that space. And certainly, the year after that, I think they'll be in what would be kind of an industry acceptable level on the yields.

In Q4, CEO Lip Bu Tan made the following remarks about margins (emphasis added):

In the short-term, I am disappointed that we are not able to fully meet the demand in our markets. My team and I are working tirelessly to drive efficiency and more output from our fabs, and while yields are in-line with our internal plans, they are still below where I want them to be. Accelerating yield improvement will be an important lever in 2026 as we look to better support our customers.

At the very least, I want to see a slight improvement in yields this quarter. How can you tell yields are improving? Well, I would closely monitor the Q2 2026 gross margin guidance vs the realized margin in Q1.

As for the Panther Lake, I admit I was impressed to see that the Intel Core Ultra Series 3 (i.e., Panther Lake) is now expected to power more than 200 OEM designs. At CES 2026, Intel announced that the Core Ultra Series 3 systems will be available globally starting Jan 27, 2026, with more designs through H1 2026. Overall, I feel optimistic about the Panther Lake given that at CES, Intel highlighted a performance of 50 NPU TOPS. In comparison, Microsoft's Copilot+ PC requires "only" 40 TOPS for its edge AI features.

However, even if the performance of the Panther Lake can be enough to run AI on the edge, if yields are not improving, the gross margin problem will persist and keep pressuring shares.

Foundry Still Under Pressure

Let me start with price action first.

Since Q3 earnings, up until the release of Q4 results, Intel was up 40%. Almost 10 percentage points of that came from the jump on January 21, on pretty much no news aside from the macro backdrop improving after Trump said in Davos he is not planning to take over Greenland by ​force.

At the time of writing this article, shares are down approximately 15% from the close on January 22. Yet, Intel is still the second stock in the foundry space in terms of 12-month returns.

Bear in mind that over the same period, Intel Foundry reported an operating loss of $10.3B, slightly better than in 2024, but not enough to call it a turnaround.

I am not convinced that Intel is out of the woods with its foundry segment. According to Reuters, Nvidia tested the 18A but decided not to go forward with this manufacturing process.

On the positive side, the company disclosed $222 million in external foundry revenue during Q4, mainly driven by projects with the U.S. government and the deconsolidation of Altera. From a guidance perspective, management guided overall Intel Foundry revenue to grow double-digits sequentially. So, the improvements in the top line are fine. The issue that I see is concerning the bottom line of this segment due to the yield problem on the 18A.

On timing, management reaffirmed in Q3 2025 the goal for Foundry to reach run-rate break-even as it exits 2027. They didn't reaffirm this target in Q4, however, management said that Foundry met key 18A and 14A milestones in the quarter. Improving yields is a slow process, and until 2027, I believe the risk of more selloffs like the one we've seen on Friday is high.

Valuation

Given the cash sink problem (i.e., Intel Foundry), we can't use the forward P/E multiple, given that the Street expects a negative EPS next year.

That said, on a forward EV/Sales basis, you may be surprised to know that, despite the massive run in the last 12 months, Intel is cheaper than Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM).

Conclusion

I find it quite strange that the market looked past a key point: data center demand was so strong that the company couldn’t keep up with supply!

Up until Q4 results, I never believed that Intel would be supply-constrained in its DCAI segment. My concern was always from a demand perspective, given the dominance of AMD and Nvidia in the AI data center space.

I am glad Intel has this problem, and I think the setup from Q2 onwards is favorable if the company is able to ramp up production and meet the high demand for its data center server CPU (Granite Rapids).

However, until then, I prefer to remain on the sidelines. From a price action perspective, I believe shares could be in for another 10% drop until they retest a long term uptrend.

Now, a $5 drop is not enough to downgrade to a Sell, and given the excitement around this stock after the Nvidia, SoftBank(SFTBY), and U.S. Government stakes, I believe a recovery is likely if Q2 results show an improvement in margins (from better yields on the 18A) and revenue.

data.title: PayPal Looks Dirt Cheap, But It May Never Recover Its Mojo (Downgrade)
data.description:
This can't be right, can it? The stock of PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) is down >35% in the past one year as I pen this update. And all this while, the market (SPX) (SPY) continues scaling new highs, and the financial sector peers (XLF) also took out new peaks in early January before the recent volatility.

I had thought that PYPL's well-battered low earnings multiples should have at least helped stem selling pressure by the pessimists from inflicting more pain, but that hasn't quite been the case. The market does what it wants to do, and the breakdown of PYPL to retest the key support level of $55 has likely stunned PayPal investors. I wouldn't be surprised if some of them think that it could be time to move on from the fintech leader. I've been scrutinizing the market tape in PYPL, and I could hardly say anything positive about it, as the disconnect with the broader financial sector and even fintech peers like Affirm (AFRM) or SoFi (SOFI) indicates that investors don't seem to have the same level of conviction they used to demonstrate back in early January 2025. That optimism has been completely eviscerated, as the company enters the final couple of weeks before PayPal's Q4 earnings release in early February.

In my previous update, while I noted that PYPL had faced “persistent headwinds,” I placed my confidence in the turnaround based on its underlying business that's still printing solid margins. However, the compression that has slashed PYPL's multiples to just over 10x at the current juncture could require a new paradigm when evaluating whether the fundamental tenets of PayPal's core business are still as robust as before. Unfortunately, after thinking through these aspects, I must highlight that there's really no clear answer, as the uncertainties in the current landscape appear to have weakened PayPal's clout, and these aren't just coming from their traditional rivals in e-commerce or fintech, particularly with the unbranded competitors.

In this update, I will discuss some of the salient challenges that could stymie the efforts of dip-buyers trying to catch this persistent falling knife situation and also enunciate whether it's still opportune for them to do so, moving forward.

Following the margins scare in the early December conference where management shared their inclination to reinvest for growth, buyers haven't shown enough conviction on why they should help the stock bottom. I think the market is certainly seeking more clarity into the margins guide for 2026 as the fintech leader braces itself for the upcoming fourth quarter earnings update. An astute commentary by the JPMorgan team made it clear why “turnaround” themes are so hard to nail with high accuracy, specifically when contemplating the PayPal thesis. And, because of so much going on right now in the fintech space, the time factor for the recovery to pan out has also gotten a lot more convoluted. In other words, the fundamental underpinnings in PayPal's story appear to have shifted resolutely against it.

Why? I think there are two key aspects that we must think about carefully when assessing the PayPal narrative: “pricing power, (and) strong incremental margins.” Despite still owning a massive two-sided merchant and consumer ecosystem, can the fintech leader still create enough synergies through its platform moat to drive additional operating leverage? That's a crucial question for investors to ponder about, because payment platforms are considered “highly scalable.” And PayPal's operations have always been strongly built around certain key growth pillars, with e-commerce being a well-established segment that has underlined its clout.

However, we know that there are significant upheavals that have been taking place in the e-commerce space. Agentic commerce is becoming a crucial interface that could reshape e-commerce economics, and no one knows exactly how it could affect PayPal's margins. After all, if increasingly more e-commerce were to take place within the more dominant AI platforms, I think there's an increasing likelihood that PayPal's pricing power could be constrained by the evolving battle between the AI shopping surfaces and how PayPal's branded checkout could be marginalized even as it adopts the AI checkouts with some of the leading models that include Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and previously OpenAI (OPENAI). However, we also know that this doesn't quite confer PayPal a distinct or sustainable moat, because the engagements and shopping activity will shift into these AI platforms, arguably bestowing the beneficial network effects on these AI companies, but not quite on PayPal.

Yet, that doesn't mean that PayPal cannot move quickly in capturing these shifting paradigms to lower the chances that it gets locked out of these burgeoning walled gardens. One clear thing is that these AI services want to become the dominant platforms for people to do their e-commerce transactions, their shopping, and probably everything else in between. So, payments will likely become increasingly commoditized, but the trust quotient that has been firmly anchored will not be quickly dissolved.

That credibility that PayPal has established with its customers and merchants, creating that lucrative two-sided network effect, should stay strong. Hence, I'm less concerned about PayPal losing these merchants/customers quickly to its key rivals (or new competitors) as compared to the structural weakening of its pricing levers. In other words, PayPal's fundamental strengths in having a huge base haven't been eroded yet, with the active accounts holding firmly at 438M, a slight increase from the 435M that it reported back in December of 2022.

And note that PayPal has also been forthcoming and reacting quickly to these transformative developments. Just yesterday (January 22), the company announced that it has “agreed to acquire Cymbio, a multi-channel orchestration platform that helps brands sell across agentic surfaces.” These developments align closely with the initiatives to broaden the PayPal brand across agentic commerce, and resonate with the nascent opportunities that are coming to companies with a more complete “suite of solutions that allows merchants to attract customers and drive sales in the new era of AI-powered commerce.”

Also, recent commentary by Amazon CEO Andy Jassy (AMZN) at Davos encapsulated the challenges facing the nascent agentic commerce right now, suggesting that such a transition will not be seamless, much less automatic:

And I think agents are going to help customers with that type of discovery. And it’s part of why we’ve invested so much in Rufus, which is our shopping assistant, which has really gotten quite good. And I think that over time that we will work with other third-party agents as well. I think today the experience hasn’t been great yet. You know, I think that a lot of these third-party agents, they don’t have your buying history, they don’t have what you like, a lot of the information about pricing and the product is off. But over time, I do believe that will get better. I also think there needs to be the right value exchange between the agents and between the retailers themselves, but I am optimistic that those will work out. We’re having conversations with lots of people and I’m very bullish on agentic commerce. - The Information interview with Andy Jassy at Davos

Yet, the reticence in allowing third-party AI agents to “infiltrate” Amazon's owned properties also demonstrates to me the inherent fear that AMZN management has when considering the potential leakages (whether it's margins, pricing power, or customer engagement/stickiness, etc.) to other AI companies looking to monetize agentic commerce as they gain even more MAUs onto their platforms. In other words, the level of uncertainty in this space is intensifying even as I pen this update, lowering the long-term conviction of investors that are still thinking about buying PYPL on the “cheap,” while waiting patiently for a recovery later.

And then, we have to think about what the ramifications are from President Trump's new call to restrict credit card interest rates to 10%. While there is a general consensus that it could help lift the BNPL side of the business, in which PayPal is one of the major players, it could have a more severe impact if these are implemented sector-wide, as the effects on the economy are expected to be wide-ranging and, most notably, damaging.

And, as if the AI disruption risks and unknowns on PayPal's business model weren't enough, the company now needs to navigate potential economy-wide headwinds that probably wouldn't do any favors for its valuation re-rating opportunities. And since e-commerce transactions are largely assessed to be discretionary, PayPal could be in for troubling times, as policy uncertainties dominate the current season. We may get more salient updates from management when Q4 earnings come into view, but I think investors have justifiably braced for the worst outcomes to arrive.

That said, PayPal's margins are still expected to hold steady through the next two years. Hence, while the growth in operating leverage is expected to remain tepid, it's not as if PayPal's pricing levers and P&L will definitely crater and then implode into oblivion. That just doesn't make sense, and I also don't expect the 438M active accounts to suddenly make a run for the exits unless justified, as these have proved to be especially sticky through the economic cycles.

Therefore, I believe the pessimism is largely predicated on the fears that have hit the sector recently, while investors worried about the effects from AI also need more time to buy into PYPL's turnaround. Yet, with PYPL's forward earnings multiples of just above 10x straddling over the historic bottom of 9.4, it seems to me that whatever growth premium that PYPL used to command has all but disappeared.

Therefore, I think that the risk/reward is now skewed too negatively, so unless a hugely disappointing guide were to surface in early February, the damaging slide should find a bottom soon. Nevertheless, while I would have normally leaned into a mean-reversion play, I think the market has shown me enough about the ambiguities facing PayPal right now and how its platform moat is getting dissected. Thus, while a bearish rating may be too downcast as PYPL is now oversold, staying long-term bullish on a losing foundational proposition no longer seems to be the right thing to do, notwithstanding the valuation appeal.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: Devon Energy Has Continued Room To Grow
data.description:
Devon Energy (NYSE: DVN) is a hydrocarbon company worth almost $24 billion. The company has an incredibly focused U.S.-based portfolio, with double-digit cash flow production and a commitment to generating strong shareholder returns in a variety of markets. It's slightly outperformed the market since we last recommended investing but has continued growth room.

As we'll see, strong share price weakness since 2022 makes the company a valuable investment.

Devon Energy Q3 Highlights

Devon Energy had a strong Q3 with impressive performance across the board.

The company delivered 390k barrels/day, near top-end guidance. At the same time, the company managed to do that with a capital investment of $860 million, 5% below guidance. That's led to strong mid-single-digit improvement in operating cost. The company is ahead of schedule on cost optimizations, which stack with this increased efficiency.

Putting all this together, the company has been able to drive substantial shareholder returns. It's retired debt ahead of schedules and reduced its outstanding share count by 13% in just a few years. The company has continued to look at opportunistic add-ons to improve its asset portfolio and, for 2026, expects to achieve further efficiency.

Devon Energy had more than $2 billion in FCF in 3 quarters, putting it at a double-digit FCF yield. The scale of the companies optimization improvements is visible in the chart above. It's exceedingly important to see a company that can outperform in every segment and carry that down to a bottom line in terms of cash flow and shareholder returns.

Devon Energy Portfolio Improvements

Breaking down the company's portfolio improvements, Devon Energy has been focused on driving strong cash flow.

Devon Energy has consistently reduced its capital spending from its 2024 guidance by a stunning $425 million as efficiency has improved. Its preliminary 2026 guidance of $3.6 billion shows a further ability to improve that number further. At the same time, the company has walked up its production guidance by almost 3% to 388 thousand barrels/day.

That double-digit capital efficiency improvement means substantial improvement in FCF / barrel, perfect for a tough environment.

Devon Energy's initiatives and continued improvements are visible above. Capital efficiency has seen $225 million in improvements for 2025, and the company is continuing to chase additional opportunities. Across the board, the company has roughly $1 billion in opportunities to reduce its costs, which should help drive additional returns.

Devon Energy Financial Metrics

Looking at Devon Energy, the company is well positioned to drive additional shareholder returns.

In 3Q 2025, the company had $820 million in FCF, annualized at ~15%. The company managed to buy back shares at a 4% rate and pay a dividend equivalent to a 2.5% yield for shareholders. That's a high single-digit return. The company managed to do that while retiring $485 million of debt, or 2% of its market cap.

That enables the company to save on debt and provide additional shareholder returns.

Devon Energy has a 0.9x net debt-to-EBITDAX with $2.5 billion of absolute debt expected to be retired. With a $1.3 billion cash position, the company can comfortably afford that, and its recent payment is a great start in that direction. The company has a strong investment-grade credit rating. Clearing up the balance sheet will save on interest costs in a high-yield environment.

It'll also enable the company to focus on additional shareholder returns past what's already an impressive rate of return.

Devon Energy 2026 Outlook

Devon Energy has released a preliminary 2026 outlook that looks great.

The company's breakeven funding level, which includes its dividend, is at <$45/barrel. Total capital is expected to come in at $3.6 billion which includes production growth versus simply maintaining production. At $60 WTI, which is around current prices, the company has an impressive 13% FCF yield, and at $70 WTI, it grows to 20% FCF yield.

Devon Energy and Coterra

Another major potential development is rumors that Coterra Energy and Devon Energy are merging.

This could make for a $50 billion megacap oil company, a combined shale company. Coterra Energy has been under an activist push to revamp the company, but a potential merger here could enable substantial cost savings and shareholder returns. We cannot speculate further until seeing the merger agreement, but given Devon Energy's strong culture, we're optimistic of management's ability to pick an effective deal.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices and natural gas prices. Natural gas is doing quite well, benefiting from artificial intelligence datacenter demand; however, that's not guaranteed to continue. A downturn there could have a strong impact on natural gas prices. Similarly, trade wars, etc., could hurt oil prices, hurting shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Devon Energy has an impressive portfolio of assets. More so, they have a management team that doesn't overrun their bounds. Instead, Devon Energy focuses on modestly expanding production while focusing even more on reducing cost per barrel to improve FCF. At the same time, Devon Energy has impressively grown its FCF.

The company has focused on strong shareholder returns for years. It's maintained a very respectable 2.5% dividend, and it's continued to aggressively repurchase shares. A double-digit reduction in its float means stronger future returns. The Coterra merger remains an open question, but overall we see strong potential.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: NMAI: Diversified Portfolio At A Hefty Discount, But Mixed Results
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The last time we gave Nuveen Multi-Asset Income Fund (NMAI) a look, it was still quite a young fund, less than a year old. That said, it was the combination of a few funds that merged into this "new" closed-end fund. This CEF provides significant diversification through investing in a hybrid portfolio that includes equity and fixed-income securities. This provides significant diversification to the managers of this fund, being able to invest where and how much they want into whatever they feel is the best opportunity.

The fund slid to a substantially wide discount as we noted in our initial coverage. That discount has narrowed since some of the widest levels, but it remains trading at near double-digit discount levels. With that, it still has some appeal to consider for some investors, but this looks to be about what the "normal" discount level could be. That would generally mean it isn't looking like too much of a bargain today, but also not overly expensive.

NMAI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.48
  • Discount/Premium: -9.09%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.63%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.44%
  • Leverage: 30.48%
  • Managed Assets: $688.2 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

NMAI's investment objective is "to provide attractive total return through high current income and capital appreciation."

To achieve this, the fund "dynamically invests in a portfolio of equity and debt securities of issuers located around the world. This dynamic investment strategy uses a risk-based framework in which any amount can be allocated to an asset class at any time. The Fund may invest in equity and debt securities of any type without limit. The relative allocations of the Fund's managed assets for investment between equity and debt securities, and relative allocations to the different types of equity and income strategies, will vary from time to time, consistent with the Fund's investment objective."

Recently, the fund managers have seen a shake-up; however, neither the investment policies nor the investment objective has been altered.

Effective 07 Jan 2026, Mark Zheng, James Kim and John Tribolet became portfolio managers of the Fund. Nathan Shetty and Anurag Dugar are no longer managers of the Fund. This update is not expected to impact the overall investment strategy.

The fund is leveraged, and that's through both borrowings and reverse repurchase agreements, both of which come with financing costs that are based on short-term borrowings. Therefore, as the Fed cuts its benchmark rate, the cost of financing has been gradually coming down. However, leverage always increases risks due to increased volatility and downside potential. It also comes with those expenses that increase the fund's overall total expense ratio to 2.98%.

Performance - Discount Narrows But Remains Rather Wide

Since our last write-up, the fund's total returns have been fairly solid but have underperformed the S&P 500 Index.

However, given the dynamic hybrid approach, the fund considers a blended benchmark that includes 50% MSCI All Country World Index, 35% Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Bond Index and 15% Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. That helps to reflect that the fund is generally 50% positioned in common stocks and the remainder in fixed-income instruments, and provides a better overall barometer for benchmarking.

They provide the annualized performance comparison in annual and semi-annual reports, which can make them a bit dated. However, what we can see in the latest semi-annual report is that the fund has performed fairly respectably against its blended benchmark on a total NAV return basis in the last one-year period. On the other hand, the fund's performance since its inception on both the share price and the NAV was rather weak overall.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results, but these are not results to necessarily brag about anyway. At the same time, the latest results appear to be a bit more encouraging for the fund.

During this period, the fund's share price was negatively impacted relative to the NAV performance due to a sizeable discount opening up. That has since narrowed, but it still remains relatively wide compared to all CEF discount averages that come in at -5.2%. For hybrid/balanced funds, the average is a bit wider at -7.9%—and NMAI is currently wider than that.

So while the fund is trading at a relatively narrower discount compared to its own average, it could still be viewed as relatively attractive. In terms of rank performance against its peers that CEFData lists, we see that the fund has been fairly mixed. Coming in fairly strong over the last year, but then the 3-year period was a fairly weaker performer in terms of total NAV returns and total share price results.

Saba Capital Management holds a position in this fund, but they had been regularly selling out of the fund in 2024 and 2025. That's left them with a fairly minimal ownership percentage of less than 5% most recently. Therefore, that is one less pressure on the fund as of late.

Distribution - Looking Enticing, But Long-Term Sustainability Questioned

The fund initially launched with a quarterly distribution policy but more recently switched to a monthly payer. This tends to be more appealing to income investors and can generate more interest. However, the fund also reduced its distribution recently, too, which is a negative sign for most investors. For me, when funds pay high distribution rates and they adjust the payout from time to time, it doesn't necessarily bother me, but it can be a catalyst to keep a fund trading at a wide discount for even longer due to others' sentiment.

In general, the fund's 9.66% NAV rate is a bit of a yellow flag. This is the level that the underlying portfolio has to earn to 'cover' the distribution and not see the NAV erode. One benefit of a wide discount is that the share price distribution yield then works out to an even more attractive 10.63%.

That said, the fund doesn't only have to earn 9.66% but also the operating expenses of 1.44% on top of that. That would push us to ~11% returns being required, and to consistently do that after a very strong last few years in the market, becomes more difficult to achieve.

The fixed-income sleeve helps to generate some relative stability in terms of coverage, as interest/dividends being received can be more predictable. With the latest semi-annual report, the fund's NII coverage came to almost 39%—which was up fairly meaningfully from the ~22%. As leverage costs ease, NII will also likely creep a bit higher from here, too.

The fund's distribution has tended to be characterized by large classifications of return of capital. This would be both destructive and non-destructive, depending on the period.

When the fund initially launched, it saw losses come quite quickly based on the higher rate environment. That resulted in NAV/share price losses quite rapidly, but along with that also came capital loss carryforwards. So even in a year when the NAV rose or stayed flat in the last couple of years, we are still seeing ROC distributions.

NMAI's Portfolio

The overall asset allocation of the fund invests primarily in common stocks as the largest breakdown of the portfolio. However, at just under 50%, it means it is fairly split. It is simply that the fixed-income sleeve is broken up even more granularly, which carries corporate bonds, asset-backed securities, senior loans, preferred, as well as other various smaller allocations.

Several years ago, when we gave the fund a look, it was similarly positioned, so this would appear to be where the fund managers felt the most comfortable. With a new management team on board, we'll have to pay attention to see if this shifts at all moving forward. As we know, the investment policy has not changed, so I suspect allocations will still remain fairly similar in the years to come.

Geographically speaking, we have seen a more material shift toward U.S. exposure over a heavier emphasis on international positioning. The latest update shows that U.S. allocation is over 70%, while previously it was under 60%. Not necessarily the most dynamic shift, but it is more noteworthy than the overall breakdown of the fund.

The United States has enjoyed some strong results over the last few years in terms of equities, but international equities outperformed in 2025.

In a look at the credit quality of the fixed-income investments, it tends to be pretty split. The below-investment-grade credit, with BB and B being fairly large allocations, is balanced by a heavier allocation to 23.8%. Junk-rated credit, including not rated, comes to 52.5%, leaving around 47.5% to higher quality of BBB and above.

The split between investment grade and below investment grade means that the fund is more sensitive to interest rate changes than compared to specifically high-yield funds, but the trade-off is also lower relative risk. In an uncertain world currently, some lower risk feels appropriate.

In looking more specifically at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG) is by far the largest allocation, though not too substantially overweight in general. With that, we can see that most holdings don't carry a heavy concentration, and that highlights just how diversified this portfolio is.

Overall, the fund lists 1,470 holdings, and that really drives home just how diversified this fund is. No single holding will really impact the results of this fund on its own; instead, it will generally be a reflection of how the broader global economics are doing.

Conclusion

NMAI trades at a meaningful discount, though narrower than its historical average level; it is wider than most of its peers. That being said, the fund has also provided some mediocre results relative to peers as well—which could make its wider relative discount appropriate. With that, the fund doesn't look excessively cheap, but neither does it look overly expensive. For investors looking for significant diversification that provides a hybrid allocation between equity and fixed income, this could be an appropriate fund.

data.title: Buda Juice: A Cold Chain Story With Hot IPO Expectations
data.description:
Lately, I've been poking around newly public names, looking for the occasional diamond the market seems to be missing.

Phoenix Education (PXED) turned out not to be a diamond, but it’s a cheap little cash generator. Rich Sparkle (ANPA) ran up triple digits without much in the way of fundamentals after jumping from financial printing into TikTok sales.

But have you ever heard of Buda Juice (BUDA)?

It's a small name that hasn't seen much attention yet, even from investors who follow IPOs closely, and after all, we're talking about a company that only went public a few weeks ago.

Buda hit the public market on January 8th, even though they’d filed way back at the end of October. They put 2 million shares out there, and the CEO, Horatio Lonsdale-Hands (also one of the founders), increased the offering by selling 666,667 of his shares—roughly a quarter of the total.

So, we’re looking at a company that:

  1. Got a really small float, which makes it quite volatile;
  2. Remains under the control of its founders, Horatio and Bernard. Meanwhile, Bryan Herr, founder of Country Fresh, is another major player, owning close to 20% of the business;
  3. Around $20 million came in from the IPO, with shares set at $7.50. The stock shot up in the first days but then leveled off, now accumulating a gain close to 5%.

Shortly after going public, Buda hit a valuation north of $106 million, even with only about $12 million in revenue TTM.

Now, if you ask me why that happened, we’ll need to take a closer look at Buda’s moat—and honestly, I’ve got a few concerns about scaling it, as you would with most organic food companies.

The Company and the Moat

There isn't a whole lot to say about Buda before it got to where it is today, except that it started out in Texas as a little kiosk selling juices and soft drinks straight to customers.

By the mid-2010s, they’d shifted to a lighter, more asset-light B2C model.

Right now, they operate with a centralized production model, churning out juices and related items under their own brand (organic and non-organic) and also for private labels, mainly sold in supermarkets throughout the Southwest.

Like I said before, the part that really matters is their proprietary UltraFresh system, although it’s apparently not patented.

This is a full-cycle solution for cold-crafted juice. From harvest to pressing, everything stays at 35°F, with no thermal pasteurization, no preservatives. Shelf life sits in the 8 to 12-day range.

It’s clearly shorter than pasteurized drinks, although still ahead of most players in this space.

For a bit of color, companies like Evolution Fresh, back when it was under Starbucks (SBUX) until 2022, relied on HPP, basically using pressure rather than heat to handle microorganisms. Buda, however, says this process changes the molecular structure and makes the juice less fresh.

Other big names like Naked Juice and Odwalla always combine cold chain processing with pasteurization, since these are mass-produced drinks with a national footprint.

And right about here, a few issues I’ve noticed start to pop into my mind.

Is UltraFresh Sustainable on a Large Scale?

First off, I’m guessing you’ve noticed by now, but Buda Juice feels less like a bet on organic juice and more like a thermal logistics company that happens to handle fresh drinks.

Even though they’re currently enjoying fat margins (31.4% EBITDA compared to 13.4% for the average staple), I figure it’s mostly because they still run a tiny operation, mostly in Texas, and haven’t invested much in infrastructure yet.

Once they scale, SG&A is probably going to shoot up.

Since we know from the IPO proceeds that they’re aiming to open plants in South Carolina, Arizona, or Nevada, I’d expect the usual growing pains (negative FCF from high Capex, higher SG&A, and a hit to profitability if sales don’t pick up).

Breaking it down, about $5 million is allocated for the South Carolina plant for improvements, equipment, and operational setup to be completed later this year, while another $5 million is targeted at expanding to the West Coast next year.

That’s less than the $20 million they pulled in from the IPO, but like I said, fixed costs such as rent will eat into profits if sales don’t pick up quickly.

Another thing that worries me is that cold logistics aren’t linear. Costs and risks shoot up quickly once you try to extend routes beyond the local cluster, and big players could copy the model.

There’s little Buda Juice can do if big players copy the model.

On a small scale, it works really well, but in my opinion, the moat fades when you try to expand.

Keeping an Eye on the Numbers and Valuation

Buda Juice saw its revenue grow at a CAGR of 41.4% through FY 2024, reaching $11.2 million in sales, although it appears to have slowed down recently.

The latest numbers we have are from Q3 FY 2025, when growth dipped from double digits in the first half of the year to around 9%, leaving TTM revenue stuck at $12.2 million.

Margins remain as Buda keeps its niche Texas operations close to home. EBITDA grew 42% year-over-year to $3.7 million in FY 2024 and is holding at $3.9 million TTM, with the margin steady above 30%.

Aside from the small slowdown and the solid margins, the balance sheet looks pretty clean.

They have no debt, so it's not like they went public to cover losses, and they've generated fairly strong FCF in recent quarters.

You can see how the asset-light model churns out cash in the graph below:

Judging by these numbers, with growth for FY 2026 likely in the high single digits, the stock seems expensive (~26.6x if we consider an EBITDA of $4 million, growing 9% year-over-year), yet the bigger error would be not factoring in the two new plants.

I'm going to imagine three scenarios here to really stress-test the valuation:

  • Scenario 1 (revenue growing 20% ​​year-over-year, EBITDA margin falling 25% to 30%): revenue of $18 million and EBITDA of $4.25 to $5.4 million.
  • Scenario 2 (revenue growing 30% year-over-year, EBITDA margin remaining at 31%): revenue of $19.5 million and EBITDA of $6 million.
  • Scenario 3 (revenue growing 50% year-over-year, EBITDA margin increased by 35%): revenue of $22.5 million and EBITDA of $7.8 million.

For peers, I went with Vita Coco (COCO), Hain Celestial (HAIN), Barfresh (BRFH), Laird Superfood (LSF), and Farmer Bros (FARM). Taken together, they’re sitting at roughly 18x EBITDA on average.

Plugging in the $2.1 million in cash and roughly 12.6 million shares outstanding, I land at a price target of about $7.05 in Scenario 1, $8.75 in Scenario 2, and roughly $11.30 in Scenario 3.

And if you look at recent deals in the beverage space like Britvic, Alani Nutrition, and Vrumona, you end up closer to about 13.5x EBITDA, which would put fair value around $6.70 if you take the midpoint of the scenarios I ran earlier.

After weighing everything, I land somewhere around $7.30 per share, splitting the difference between Scenarios 1 and 2 and what recent deals suggest.

What this tells me is that a lot of the growth is already baked into the price, leaving something like 15% downside from here and suggesting the stock came out of the IPO pretty close to fair value.

It's not quite the asymmetry you're looking for in a stock with a very low float.

The upside is there, but a lot has to go right.

Sales would need to run hotter than the old CAGR; margins can't fall apart once SG&A and operating costs start creeping up.

On the flip side, it wouldn't take much for the downside to show up. A slower and more expensive expansion would be more than enough.

I'd rather sit this one out. It's a 'Sell' for me today.

Buda isn't a bad company, but the stock itself doesn't look especially attractive at these levels.

data.title: Airbnb Suffers From Premium Valuations And Maturing Growth - Mixed Return Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Airbnb, Inc. (ABNB) in October 2025, discussing its mixed prospects entering the Q3'25 earnings season, attributed to the mixed travel indicators heading into the quarter arising from the "weaker demand growth for alternative accommodations."

Pending further growth catalysts, I had believed that the stock might continue underperforming ahead, despite the healthy balance sheet and the ongoing share retirement, resulting in my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Hold rating for the ABNB stock here, despite the growth initiatives already delivering expanding customer deposits, attributed to its mixed return prospects arising from the expensive valuations and the notable sideways trading cadence over the past few years.

This is pending an acceleration in its growth prospects to match the premium valuations and a further acceleration in its EPS growth from the ongoing share repurchases.

ABNB Outlines Growth Initiatives

Since my last Hold rating, ABNB has recorded an outsized recovery of +13.8% at its peak, before losing some of its recent gains attributed to the "new data that showed international tourism to the U.S. is weakening," with a similar selloff also observed in its travel peers in varying degrees.

I am of the opinion that the correction has been unwarranted indeed, since this is backwards-looking information for 2025, with readers well advised to look forward to the upcoming Q4'25 earnings season to hear about the consumer travel trends entering 2026.

If anything, ABNB has highlighted their ambition to be "the best one-stop shop for travel," with it perhaps explaining why the management has actively partnered with "both boutique and independent hotels" in numerous global cities, given the "huge supplement for our supply, especially in supply-constrained markets."

This move lends credence to my prior conjecture that the company may "expand its supply and active listings to drive growth," on top of offering "more value added services to deliver a one-stop shop travel booking experience, similar to other Online Travel Agencies" - in this case, hotels.

This is especially true since ABNB has reported a stable take rate of 13.3% on a YTD basis (-0.1 points YoY), based on the YTD revenues of $9.46B (+9.7% YoY) and the Gross Booking Value (GBV) of $70.9B (+10.4% YoY).

When comparing to its direct Online Travel Agency (OTA) peers, including:

  • Expedia (EXPE) at 12% on a YTD basis (-0.1 points YoY), and
  • Booking (BKNG) at 14.3% on a YTD basis (+0.1 points YoY), respectively,

it is apparent that ABNB's moderate ADRs of $171 in FQ3'25 (+4.6% YoY) against EXPE's at $210 (+1.9% YoY) and BKNG at approximately $153 (+5.5% YoY, author's estimates based on gross bookings and room nights), are not a cause of concern, with things likely to sequentially grow once hotels/services/experiences comprise a larger part of the former's booking value.

If anything, ABNB has hinted at further outperformance in FQ4'25, based on the guidance of "our implied take rate in Q4 2025 to be relatively flat year-over year," with "our GBV to grow low-double digits year-over-year. We anticipate our GBV to benefit from a modest increase in ADR, primarily due to price appreciation and FX, as well as continued growth in Nights and Seats Booked."

For reference, the company previously reported a take rate of 14.2% in FQ4'24, based on the revenues of $2.5B (+12% YoY) and the GBV of $17.6B (+13% YoY), with FY2025 likely to match FY2024 take rates of 13.5% (inline YoY).

Moving forward, ABNB has intensified their efforts to drive booking growth by offering longer lead time bookings through flexible payment schedules through:

  • the roll-out of Reserve Now, Pay Later offering within the US, with the zero-upfront payment already driving "the acceleration of Nights and Seats Booked in Q3, and we’ll continue rolling it out more broadly in the coming months," and
  • the fee free installments of up to 6 months in Brazil, with it "already accounted for nearly 50% of total bookings value in Q3."

This is on top of the heightened marketing efforts by:

  • "leveraging social partnerships in Japan,"
  • "tapping cultural insights in Korea,"
  • launching Airbnb Services and Experiences, with "almost half of the people who booked an experience did not have an Airbnb stay,"
  • expanding internationally to new cities/countries, and
  • "rolling out new social features so you can share memories with co-travelers, stay in touch with the people you meet on experiences, and manage everything with simple and transparent privacy controls," amongst others,

... which are already delivering higher funds receivable and amounts held on behalf of customers (customer deposit) at $7.2B (+9.5% YoY), with it providing readers with robust insights into its future top-line performance.

Therefore, while ABNB's FY2025 adj EBITDA margin guidance of 35% (-1 points YoY) somewhat mirrors the YTD performance of 37.1% (-0.8 points YoY) and implies its deteriorating profitability, I am not overly concerned indeed, aided by the slight raise from the prior guidance of 34.5% (-1.5 points YoY) offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call.

This is especially since the margin decline is attributed to the "investments in new growth and policy initiatives," as discussed above, with these efforts likely to trigger deepened customer engagement, expanded cross-selling, and growing OTA mindshare.

This is on top of the integration of AI to deliver a more personalized experience while reducing customers' friction - all of which are likely to trigger accelerated top/bottom-line growth.

ABNB's Maturing Growth Comes At Hefty Price Tag

The same quiet optimism has been observed in the consensus forward estimates, with ABNB expected to report excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +9.9%/+11.7% through FY2027, albeit notably decelerating compared to the 5Y historical top-line growth profile at a CAGR of +18.2% and the 2Y historical bottom-line growth at +18.8%.

Despite trading sideways over the past three years, it is apparent that ABNB remains rather expensive at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x compared to the sector median of 18.14x, despite the moderation from the 5Y mean of 52.55x.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +11.7%, it goes without saying that the stock remains inflated at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.70x, compared to the sector median of 2.31x.

A similar conclusion may also be derived when comparing ABNB's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x and 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.70x against its OTA peers, including:

  • Trip.com Group Limited (TCOM) at 9.48x/1.04x (using the same methodology),
  • EXPE at 17.86x/0.83x, and
  • BKNG at 22.59x/1.25x, respectively,

... with there remaining a minimal margin of safety here, since ABNB is likely to continue trading sideways as it grows into its premium valuations - presenting its mixed return prospects in the intermediate term.

ABNB Remains A Hold

For now, ABNB continues to trade sideways between the established support levels of the $110s since mid-2023 and the resistance levels of the $140s since early 2025, with it signaling its potential for a swing trade position before the eventual breakout.

This conjecture is not overly bearish in my view, since the stock is trading at a notable premium against my base-case fair value estimates of $74, based on the LTM EPS performance of $4.11 (+44.2% sequentially) and the sector P/E median of ~18x.

There remains a minimal margin of safety compared to my base-case long-term price target of $102.60, based on the consensus FY2027 EPS estimates of $5.70, with it further lending credence to my conjecture surrounding its mixed return prospects at current levels.

While there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +38.1% to my bull-case long-term price target of $180.60, it is contingent on ABNB sustaining its premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 31.70x moving forward - a speculative conjecture attributed to the notable moderation from historical levels and the premium against its direct peers.

This is especially since the intensified growth initiatives are multiyear strategies, likely to trigger near-term noise in its bottom-line and, consequently, volatilities in its stock price performance.

Barring an acceleration in its growth prospects to match the premium valuations and a further acceleration in its EPS growth profile from the ongoing share repurchases (-3.2% over the LTM and -9.2% from FQ2'22), I maintain my conclusion in which ABNB's near-term return prospects are likely to underwhelm, resulting in my reiterated Hold (Neutral) rating here.
